title: SEGMENT 17 — After the paper — Mila's death, the n-Lorem Foundation, the institutional response

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — After the paper — Mila's death, the n-Lorem Foundation, the institutional response
The milasen paper was published in October of 2019.
By that point, Mila had been receiving the drug for almost two years.
The paper covered the first year of clinical observations in detail; her treatment continued after the paper appeared.
Through 2019 and into 2020, milasen was administered to her on the every-three-month maintenance schedule.
The reductions in seizures were maintained.
The brain atrophy continued.
Her overall clinical status declined gradually but, by report, more slowly than the natural history of CLN7 would have predicted.
In February of 2021, Mila Makovec died.
She was ten years old.
The drug had been administered to her for approximately three years.
Her death was not unexpected, given the underlying disease, but it was, for her family and her medical team, the end of a long and extraordinary fight.

The Mila's Miracle Foundation, which her family had founded to support the research that led to her treatment, continued — and continues — to support work on individualized therapies for rare diseases, and on Batten disease specifically.
The institutional response to the milasen case was, in retrospect, more important than the individual treatment itself.
Three things, in particular, happened in the wake of the paper.
First, Boston Children's Hospital established an Oversight Committee on Personalized Experimental Therapeutics.

This is a formal institutional body that reviews proposals for individualized treatments developed at the hospital, ensuring that the scientific case, the safety package, the ethical considerations, and the patient and family engagement all meet a consistent standard.
The committee exists because, immediately after milasen, the hospital was approached by many families asking whether their child could receive a similar custom drug.
Some of those requests were appropriate.
Some were not.
Without a formal review structure, the hospital had no consistent way to decide which proposals to pursue and which to decline.
The committee provides that structure.
Second, the FDA began to develop more formal guidance for individualized ASO development.
In 2020 and 2021, the FDA issued draft guidance documents on the development of individualized ASOs for severely debilitating or life-threatening genetic diseases.

The guidance documents specify what kinds of preclinical data the FDA expects for these therapies, how the abbreviated preclinical program should be structured, what safety monitoring is required, and what the regulatory submissions should contain.
The guidance does not eliminate the FDA's case-by-case judgment, but it does provide a framework that researchers and clinicians can work within.
The milasen case was the foundational example that the guidance was developed around.
Third — and this is, in my opinion, the most significant institutional outcome of the entire story — the n-Lorem Foundation was founded.

n-Lorem is a non-profit organization founded by Stanley Crooke, who is the founder and former CEO of Ionis Pharmaceuticals, the company whose ASO chemistry was the platform for both nusinersen and milasen.

Crooke recognized, watching the milasen case, that the technical capability to develop individualized ASOs existed but that the economic and institutional infrastructure to deliver them broadly did not.
He founded n-Lorem specifically to fill that gap.
Here is what n-Lorem does.
It accepts applications from clinicians on behalf of patients with rare genetic diseases caused by mutations that, in n-Lorem's expert judgment, are likely to be amenable to ASO-based therapy.

If the case is accepted, n-Lorem designs and manufactures a custom ASO for that patient, conducts the necessary preclinical safety work, supports the FDA submission, and provides the drug to the patient — for free, indefinitely, for the rest of the patient's life.
The organization is funded by donations and by the in-kind contributions of Ionis Pharmaceuticals, which provides the technical infrastructure.
As of this lecture, n-Lorem has accepted patients into its program and produced individualized ASOs for many of them, with treatments ongoing.
The institutional achievement of n-Lorem is that it has industrialized, more or less, what milasen demonstrated could be done as a one-off heroic effort.

The case-by-case medical detective work of identifying a treatable molecular target, designing an ASO, validating it in patient cells, conducting preclinical safety studies, navigating the FDA, manufacturing the drug, and delivering it to the patient — this entire workflow has been turned, by n-Lorem, into a repeatable program.

Not for every rare disease — only for those where the molecular pathology is amenable to ASO therapy and where the case meets n-Lorem's scientific criteria — but for a meaningful subset of patients who would otherwise have no path to a treatment.
Without milasen, n-Lorem might not exist, or might exist in a very different form.
The case proved the concept.
The foundation institutionalized the workflow.
I want to name explicitly what the milasen paper accomplished, beyond its specific clinical findings, because the dominoes it knocked over have continued to fall in important ways.
First, it proved that a brand-new drug can be designed and dosed in one patient in less than one year.
Twelve months from referral to first dose.
That timeline had never been demonstrated for a brand-new drug for any disease.
It is now the working assumption for individualized ASO programs.
Second, it established that ASO chemistry is portable across diseases when the splice defect is amenable.

The nusinersen platform — the 2'-MOE phosphorothioate chemistry, the intrathecal delivery, the splice-modulating mechanism — was shown to work for a different gene, a different mutation, a different patient.
The platform is genuinely portable, and that portability is the basis of every subsequent individualized splicing therapy.
Third, it created the FDA template for individualized IND submissions.
The regulatory framework that handled milasen has become, with refinement, the framework that handles subsequent individualized ASO submissions.
The pathway exists because milasen proved it could exist.
Fourth, it triggered the rare-disease N-of-1 movement.
n-Lorem is the most visible example, but it is not the only one.
Academic medical centers have begun to develop their own individualized therapy programs.
Patient advocacy organizations have begun to fund similar efforts.

The broader idea that individual patients with rare diseases might be candidates for custom drugs has moved from "unprecedented" to "occasionally feasible," which is an enormous shift in the moral landscape of rare disease medicine.
Fifth, and finally, it showed the honest limits of the approach.
The paper did not overclaim.
It reported that the drug slowed but did not stop the disease.
It reported the MRI atrophy honestly.

It set the appropriate expectation that individualized therapies can extend or improve lives without necessarily saving them, and that the earlier they are started, the more benefit they are likely to provide.
That honesty has shaped how the field thinks about its own promise.
We are not selling cures.
We are, when we are lucky, buying time.
And time, especially for a child with a fatal disease, is not nothing — but it is also not everything we might want.
